telltalePatterns: Alveolar tap [ɾ] for English rhotic /ɹ/ (e.g., in 'river', 'red')
Voiced dental/alveolar stop [d] substitution for /ð/ (e.g., in 'the', 'weather')
Syllable-timed rhythm transfer from L1, contrasting with English stress-timed rhythm (e.g., in 'really beautiful')

phonemeProblems.produced: Alveolar tap [ɾ] instead of retroflex approximant [ɹ]
Bilabial stop [b] instead of labiodental fricative [v]

prosody.fluencyNotes: Fluent delivery with good connected speech; words are not produced in isolation. Pausing occurs appropriately at grammatical boundaries.

strengths: Clear distinction between tense /iː/ and lax /ɪ/ vowels (e.g., 'green' vs 'singing').

recommendedFocus.reason: Substituting these with /t/ and /d/ is the most significant contributor to a 'foreign' accent and can cause lexical confusion (e.g., 'three' vs. 'tree', 'then' vs. 'den').
Replacing the American /ɹ/ with a tapped /ɾ/ is a highly salient feature of the speaker's accent. Mastering the correct approximant sound would dramatically improve native-likeness.
Confusing these two phonemes (betacism) can occasionally impact intelligibility (e.g., 'very' sounding like 'berry') and is a classic L1 transfer pattern to address.

result.transcript: Yesterday the weather was really beautiful, so I decided to go for a walk through the park near my house. The trees were green and the birds were singing everywhere. I think spring is my favorite time of the year. I walked along the river and watched some children playing with a big red ball. They were laughing and having so much fun together. One little girl threw the ball very high, and it landed right in the water. Her father had to reach in and pull it out. After that, I stopped at a small village shop to buy some bread and a bottle of juice.
